onboarding.title: EVEREST EXPEDITION COMMAND BRIEFING
onboarding.author: EXPEDITION COORDINATOR
paragraphs: Welcome to Mission Everest. You have been placed with our active expedition team preparing to summit the world's highest mountain. The team is entering final preparation with a narrow weather window ahead.
Your role: Expedition Coordinator — Junior Grade.
Your team: Captain Arjun Singh, Maya Sharma, Tenzin Sherpa, Priya Nair.
Three days. One mission. The summit is earned through preparation.

messages.text: Welcome to Mission Everest. Every successful summit begins with careful preparation.
We'll make sure you're ready for high altitude.
The mountain rewards patience, not speed.

system_card.heading: BASE CAMP ORIENTATION
system_card.body: You tour Base Camp: the mess tent, medical station, comms hub, and gear bay. Priya walks you through how the team tracks readiness. Eight core metrics are now active on your dashboard. Every decision from this point forward moves them.

messages.text: Before we move, we pack. What's your approach?

reaction.text: Good. Light and disciplined. That's how the mountain is respected.
reaction.low: Acceptable. Light packs, but discipline is more than a checklist.
reaction.high: Good — you're already thinking the way I do. Light and honest with the mountain.

options.label: Carry as much equipment as possible.

reaction.text: That's more weight than the schedule accounts for. We'll need to adjust supply loads.
reaction.low: That won't work with my manifest. I'll need to reconfigure the loads before we lose the schedule.
reaction.high: Hey — that's a heavier plan than we agreed. Talk to me, I'll rebuild the loads.

reaction.text: That's the right instinct. Every gram matters up here.
reaction.low: Acceptable. Let's see if that judgment holds on the mountain.
reaction.high: That's exactly it — you're starting to think like a climber, not a tourist.

messages.text: Your body needs time to adjust. How do we approach the schedule?

reaction.text: That's how altitude sickness starts. We need to slow down.
reaction.low: No. That plan doesn't clear medical. We slow down.
reaction.high: I know you're eager, but that's how altitude sickness starts. Let's slow it down together.

reaction.text: Rest matters, but we also need to keep moving on schedule.
reaction.low: Rest is fine, but the schedule isn't optional. I'll be watching the timeline.
reaction.high: Rest matters — I love that you led with health — but we do need to keep moving.

reaction.text: Exactly right. Trust the process, not the urge to rush.
reaction.low: Correct, though I'd like to see that discipline hold up under pressure.
reaction.high: You get it. Honestly, most learners don't listen this well this early.

messages.text: We need to lock our route before nightfall. What's the call?

reaction.text: Speed isn't free. That route carries more exposure.
reaction.low: Noted. I'll be double-checking that route against weather before I sign off.
reaction.high: I hear you on the pace, but that line carries more exposure than you'd like.

options.label: Delay all movement for perfect weather.

reaction.text: We can't wait for perfect. We wait for safe enough.
reaction.low: That's overcaution. Perfect windows don't exist up here.
reaction.high: I get the instinct, but perfect windows don't exist. We move on safe enough.

options.label: Select the safest route based on weather and team readiness.

reaction.text: That's the decision I'd make. Good instincts.
reaction.low: Reasonable. I'll still cross-check the read before we lock it.
reaction.high: That's the call I'd make. Honestly, I'm glad it came from you first.

messages.text: Before we close today — what's your read on the biggest risk heading into the climb?

reaction.text: Agreed. That's the variable we control least.
reaction.low: Fine. I'll want to see how you plan around it before I fully agree.
reaction.high: Same read here. That's the variable I lose sleep over too.

reaction.text: Good read. Bodies don't lie at altitude.
reaction.low: Not wrong. I'd still like to see you back that up on the medical rounds.
reaction.high: Great read — bodies don't lie at altitude. Thanks for seeing it.

options.label: Whether we're truly prepared for the summit push.

reaction.text: That's the only question that matters right now.
reaction.low: Correct enough. Let's see if you can answer it as clearly tomorrow.
reaction.high: That's the only question that matters. Good — you're already looking up.

reflection.question: What surprised you most about expedition preparation today?

options.label: How much planning happens before any climbing starts.

response.text: Most people don't see this part. Planning is 90% of the mission.
response.low: Fair observation. Most people miss it entirely.
response.high: You noticed the part nobody credits. That's why we get to the top.

options.label: How closely health and performance are connected.

response.text: You noticed that fast. Most learners take longer.
response.low: A reasonable observation. It'll matter more tomorrow.
response.high: Honestly, I wish more climbers led with that instinct.

options.label: How every decision affects the whole team, not just me.

response.text: That's leadership. Hold onto that instinct.
response.low: That's a start. We'll see if it holds under pressure.
response.high: That's leadership — the real kind. Hold onto that instinct.
